does: This paper introduces RIFT-Bench, a dynamic red-teaming framework designed to evaluate the security of agentic AI systems built on large language models. The methodology uses graph representations to capture system architecture and operates through two automated phases: a Discovery phase that extracts the structural properties of an agentic system, and a Scanning phase that deploys adaptive adversarial attacks while generating comprehensive evaluation reports. Rather than relying on fixed attack templates, RIFT-Bench dynamically adapts its adversarial probes to the specific characteristics of each system, enabling it to test across diverse attack vectors and objectives. The authors validate their approach by evaluating 45 different agentic systems with varying implementations and demonstrate that their framework generalizes effectively across this heterogeneous landscape.
connects: This work responds to a critical gap in AI safety research where existing security evaluations for agentic systems lack standardization and are typically tied to specific implementations or application domains. Prior work on LLM vulnerabilities and jailbreaking has identified numerous attack vectors, but these methods were designed for single-turn interactions with base models rather than complex autonomous agents with tool use, planning, and memory. The paper builds on graph-based system analysis approaches used in software security while extending red-teaming methodologies beyond traditional adversarial prompt injection to account for the unique attack surfaces introduced by agent architecture—including tool abuse, goal manipulation, and information leakage across system components. RIFT-Bench synthesizes dynamic adaptation techniques from adversarial ML with structured vulnerability assessment practices.
enables: This work establishes infrastructure for comparative security benchmarking of agentic systems, enabling researchers and practitioners to systematically identify and prioritize vulnerabilities before deployment. The dynamic, architecture-agnostic evaluation approach creates a foundation for developing more robust agent designs by revealing common failure modes across implementations. Future work can extend the framework to cover additional attack vectors specific to emerging agent capabilities like multi-agent coordination or persistent learning. The graph-based representation and automated scanning pipeline may also enable development of automated defense mechanisms and real-time monitoring systems that detect similar attack patterns during deployment. This benchmark facilitates the creation of more standardized security practices across the rapidly evolving agentic AI ecosystem, supporting safer autonomous decision-making systems.
